steps.title: Mijn Familie
steps.desc: Welcome to Unit 5: family and home. You already know mijn/jouw/zijn/haar from Unit 4. Now we add the full family vocabulary and expand your possessive toolkit. By the end of this unit, you'll be able to talk about your people, your house, and your daily life.

steps.note: De-word. International cognate — same in almost every European language.

De-word. Dutch v = English f — a regular sound shift.
vader/father, vis/fish, vinden/find, vol/full, vier/four.
This is one of the most useful patterns in Dutch:
when you see 'v-' in Dutch, try 'f-' in English.
Informal: papa.

De-word. Same Germanic root as English/German.
Informal: mama.

De-word. Plural: broers.
Same root as English 'brother'.

De-word. Plural: zussen.
Also: 'de zuster' (more formal/literary).

steps.also: ⚠️ False friend: Dutch 'kind' = child, NOT English 'kind' (= aardig)
steps.example: Het kind is drie jaar oud.
steps.exampleEn: The child is three years old.
steps.note:
Het-word. Plural: kinderen (irregular).
False friend! English 'kind' = Dutch 'aardig'.
But in Old English, 'kind' DID mean 'child' — same word.
You can still see it in English 'kindergarten' (children's garden)
and 'humankind' (the human child/offspring → humanity).
English shifted the meaning; Dutch and German kept it.

De-word. Plural: zonen.
Same root as English 'son' and German 'Sohn'.

De-word. Plural: dochters.
The 'ch' is the Dutch throat sound.
Same root as English 'daughter'.

steps.example: Mijn opa is tachtig jaar oud.
steps.exampleEn: My grandfather is eighty years old.
steps.note:
De-word. Informal and universal.
Formal: de grootvader. Nobody says that.

De-word. Same as German.
Formal: de grootmoeder. Rarely used.

steps.title: 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 Family Vocabulary Overview
steps.text:
de vader / papa = father / dad
de moeder / mama = mother / mum
de broer = brother
de zus = sister
de zoon = son
de dochter = daughter
het kind = child (plural: kinderen)
de opa = grandpa
de oma = grandma
de man = husband (also: man)
de vrouw = wife (also: woman)

Notice: almost ALL family words are de-words.
Exception: het kind (het-word).
You already know 'man' and 'vrouw' from Unit 1!

steps.note:
Past participle of 'trouwen' (to marry).
Shares a root with English 'betrothed' and 'troth.'
'To plight one's troth' = to pledge one's faithfulness.
English 'troth' became rare, surviving mainly in wedding
vows. Dutch kept it as the everyday word for marriage.
'Getrouwd met' = married to.

steps.example: Mijn ouders wonen in Den Haag.
steps.exampleEn: My parents live in The Hague.
steps.note:
Literally: 'the olds' (from 'oud' = old).
Always plural: ouders.
Same logic as English 'elders'.

steps.title: 🇳🇱 Dutch Family Culture
steps.text:
Dutch families are typically smaller than in many countries.
One or two children is standard.

The Dutch value independence — children are raised to be
'zelfstandig' (self-reliant) from a young age.

'Doe maar normaal, dan doe je al gek genoeg.'
= Just be normal, that's crazy enough.
This is THE Dutch parenting motto.
Don't show off. Don't be dramatic. Just be normal.

Families often live close but not together.
Sunday lunch at opa and oma's is a common tradition.
'Op bezoek bij opa en oma' = Visiting grandpa and grandma.

steps.q: Which is the ONLY het-word in the family?
steps.hint: Children are special in Dutch grammar too

steps.s: {1} moeder is lerares. {2} vader werkt in Utrecht.
steps.hint: HER mother is a teacher. MY father works in Utrecht.

steps.s: Mijn opa en oma ___ in Groningen.

steps.q: 'Doe maar normaal' reflects the Dutch value of:
steps.hint: Don't show off, just be normal

steps.title: Ons Huis
steps.desc: Time to complete the possessives. You know mijn/jouw/zijn/haar/uw from Unit 4. Now: ons/onze (our), jullie (your plural), and hun (their). Plus the rooms and objects that make a Dutch house a Dutch house.

steps.note:
'Our' has TWO forms in Dutch!
'Ons' for het-words: ons huis, ons kind.
'Onze' for de-words: onze vader, onze kat.
This is the only possessive that changes.

'Onze' for de-words and ALL plurals:
onze vader, onze kinderen, onze huizen.
'Ons' ONLY for singular het-words.

steps.title: 📐 Ons vs Onze — The Rule
steps.text:
This is the ONLY possessive that changes form:

ONS + singular het-word:
ons huis = our house
ons kind = our child
ons glas = our glass

ONZE + de-word or plural:
onze vader = our father
onze tuin = our garden
onze kinderen = our children
onze huizen = our houses

Every other possessive stays the same:
mijn huis / mijn vader (no change)
jouw huis / jouw vader (no change)

Only 'our' splits into ons/onze.

steps.note:
You already know 'jullie' as a pronoun (you all).
As possessive: 'jullie huis' = your house.
No change for de/het — always 'jullie'.

'Hun' = their. No change for de/het.
Hun huis, hun vader, hun kinderen.
Simple and consistent.

Het-word. So: 'ons huis' (not 'onze').
Plural: huizen (s → z voicing).

De-word. Plural: kamers.
Slaapkamer = bedroom, woonkamer = living room.

De-word. Same root as English 'kitchen'.
The heart of a Dutch home — small but functional.

steps.example: Onze tuin is klein maar mooi.
steps.exampleEn: Our garden is small but beautiful.
steps.note:
De-word. So: 'onze tuin' (not 'ons').
Dutch gardens are typically tiny but immaculate.

De-word. Plural: trappen.
Dutch stairs are FAMOUSLY steep — nearly vertical.
Moving a couch? Use the hook on the gable.

Het-word. So: 'ons raam'.
Dutch windows are enormous.
And often WITHOUT curtains. On purpose.

steps.title: 🇳🇱 The Dutch House
steps.text:
Dutch houses are... unique.

Steep stairs: nearly vertical, notoriously narrow.
Moving furniture? Use the hook (hijsbalk) on the roof peak
and hoist it through the window.

Big windows, NO curtains: the Dutch tradition of open windows
comes from Calvinist culture — 'we have nothing to hide.'
You can see straight into people's living rooms. This is normal.

Rijtjeshuizen: row houses. The typical Dutch street is a long
row of attached houses, each narrow and tall (3-4 floors).

The tuin is small — often just a tiny patch behind the house.
But it will be perfect. The Dutch are champion gardeners.

'Gezellig' lives in the woonkamer (living room).
Small, warm, filled with candles.

opts: it's a rule for all possessives

steps.q: Which possessive is the ONLY one with two forms?
steps.hint: Every other possessive stays the same

steps.s: {1} huis is groot. {2} kinderen zijn klein.

steps.title: Elke Ochtend
steps.desc: You can't describe a morning without reflexive verbs. 'I wash myself, I dress myself, I feel good.' Dutch uses these MORE than English — 'voelen' is always 'zich voelen'. If you speak German, French, Spanish, or Romanian, you already know the concept.

steps.title: 🪞 What Are Reflexive Verbs?
steps.text:
A reflexive verb is one where the subject acts on ITSELF:

Ik was me. = I wash myself.
Jij kleedt je aan. = You dress yourself.
Hij voelt zich goed. = He feels (himself) good.

English often drops the 'myself' part:
'I wash' (myself is implied).
'I feel good' (myself is implied).

Dutch NEVER drops it:
Ik was me. ✅ (me = myself)
Ik was. ❌ (this means 'I was' — past tense of zijn!)

The reflexive pronoun changes per person:
ik → me
jij → je
hij/zij → zich
wij → ons
jullie → je
zij (they) → zich

deepDive.title: If you speak other languages...
deepDive.text:
French: je me lave, tu te laves, il se lave
Spanish: me lavo, te lavas, se lava
German: ich wasche mich, du wäschst dich, er wäscht sich
Romanian: mă spăl, te speli, se spală

Dutch works exactly the same way.
The reflexive pronoun sits after the verb:
Ik was me. (I wash myself)
Jij wast je. (You wash yourself)
Hij wast zich. (He washes himself)

If you already know reflexives in ANY Romance
or Germanic language, Dutch reflexives are free.

steps.note:
Reflexive verb. Dictionary form: 'zich wassen'.
'Zich' = oneself (placeholder).
Conjugated: ik was me, jij wast je, hij wast zich.

steps.example: Ik was me om zeven uur.
steps.exampleEn: I wash myself at seven o'clock.
steps.note:
was = stam of wassen. me = myself.
⚠️ 'Ik was' alone = I was (past tense of zijn).
'Ik was me' = I wash myself. Context is everything.

wast = stam + t. zich = himself.
For hij/zij/het, the reflexive pronoun is 'zich'.
'Zich' is like French 'se' or German 'sich'.

Reflexive + SEPARABLE! Two things at once.
aankleden → ik kleed me aan.
'aan' goes to the end. Reflexive pronoun stays with the verb.

In Dutch, 'voelen' is ALWAYS reflexive for emotions.
'I feel good' = Ik voel ME goed.
Never just 'Ik voel goed' — that's incomplete.

Regular reflexive. Stam = scheer.
'sch' = the Dutch 'sg' sound.
The 'zich' makes it clear: he shaves HIMSELF.

steps.example: Ik douche me om kwart over zeven.
steps.exampleEn: I shower at quarter past seven.
steps.note:
From French 'douche'. Regular verb.
Stam = douche. Ik douche me, jij doucht je.

steps.example: Ik word elke dag om zes uur wakker.
steps.exampleEn: I wake up every day at six o'clock.
steps.note:
Not reflexive, but SEPARABLE: wakker...worden.
'Ik word wakker' = I wake up. 'Wakker' goes to the end.
Verb 'worden' = to become. You 'become awake'.

steps.title: 📋 Reflexive Pronouns — Full List
steps.text:
ik → me (myself)
jij / je → je (yourself)
u → zich / u (yourself formal)
hij → zich (himself)
zij → zich (herself)
het → zich (itself)
wij → ons (ourselves)
jullie → je (yourselves)
zij → zich (themselves)

Pattern: me, je, zich, ons, je, zich.
'Zich' is the default for 3rd person and formal 'u'.
'Je' does double duty for jij AND jullie.

steps.title: 🌅 A Dutch Morning
steps.text:
A typical Dutch morning:

Ik word om zes uur wakker. = I wake up at 6.
Ik douche me. = I shower.
Ik was me. = I wash myself.
Ik scheer me. = I shave.
Ik kleed me aan. = I get dressed.
Ik voel me goed! = I feel good!

Ik eet een boterham met kaas. = I eat a sandwich with cheese.
Ik drink een kopje koffie. = I drink a cup of coffee.

Notice: reflexive pronouns always sit right after the verb.
Subject + verb + reflexive pronoun + rest.

steps.q: Why must Dutch always include 'me' in 'Ik was me'?
steps.hint: ik was = I was. ik was me = I wash myself.
ik kleed ME AAN — two things at once

steps.s: Ik douche {1} om zeven uur. Hij scheert {2} elke ochtend.
steps.hint: I shower MYSELF. He shaves HIMSELF.

steps.q: 'Ik word om zes uur wakker' — where does 'wakker' go?

steps.title: De Grote Rode Fiets
steps.desc: You've used adjectives casually — groot, klein, mooi, lekker. Now learn the SYSTEM. Dutch adjectives add -e... most of the time. There's one exception, and it trips up even advanced learners. This is the most important grammar rule in this unit.

steps.title: 📐 The Adjective -e Rule
steps.text:
When an adjective goes BEFORE a noun, it usually adds -e:

de grote tuin = the big garden ✅
het kleine huis = the small house ✅
een mooie kamer = a beautiful room ✅

But there is ONE exception:

een groot huis = a big house ❌ (no -e!)

The exception: een/geen + het-word + adjective = NO -e.

DE-WORDS: always add -e.
de grote tuin ✅
een grote tuin ✅

HET-WORDS: add -e with de/het/dit/dat. NO -e with een/geen.
het grote huis ✅
een groot huis ✅ (no -e!)

This is the ONLY exception. Everything else gets -e.

deepDive.title: Why this exception exists
deepDive.text:
Historical grammar reason:

Old Dutch had three genders: masculine, feminine, neuter.
De-words are masculine/feminine. Het-words are neuter.

With definite articles (de, het), ALL genders got -e.
With indefinite articles (een), neuter (het) kept the bare form.

So 'een groot huis' is a fossil from Old Dutch gender marking.

German still has the full system:
ein großes Haus, eine große Küche, der große Garten...
Dutch simplified it to just one exception.

If you speak German: Dutch is German's adjective system
with 90% of the complexity removed.

steps.example: De grote tuin. Een groot huis.
steps.exampleEn: The big garden. A big house.
steps.note:
De-word: de grote tuin, een grote tuin. (always -e)
Het-word: het grote huis (definite → -e)
BUT: een groot huis (indefinite het → NO -e!)

steps.example: Het kleine kopje. Een klein kopje.
steps.exampleEn: The small cup. A small cup.
steps.note:
klein → kleine (with -e) or klein (without).
Het kleine kopje (definite het → -e).
Een klein kopje (indefinite het → no -e).

steps.example: De mooie kamer. Een mooie kamer.
steps.exampleEn: The beautiful room. A beautiful room.
steps.note:
De-word: always -e → mooie.
mooi → mooie: the 'i' just gets an '-e' added.
Een mooie kamer ✅ (de-word = always -e).

steps.example: Het oude huis. Een oud huis.
steps.exampleEn: The old house. An old house.
steps.note:
Het oude huis (definite → -e).
Een oud huis (indefinite het → no -e).
Final -d sounds like -t (devoicing).

nieuw → nieuwe. De-word = always -e.
Identical cognate across Germanic languages.

rood → rode. The 'oo' becomes 'o' (open syllable).
Same Foundations rule: ro-de = open syllable → long vowel.
One 'o' is enough.

steps.title: 🎯 The Decision Tree
steps.text:
Before a noun, ask:

1. Is it a DE-word?
   → YES: add -e. Always. Done.
   de grote tuin, een grote tuin.

2. Is it a HET-word?
   → With het/dit/dat/ons: add -e.
     het grote huis, dit kleine raam.
   → With een/geen/no article: NO -e.
     een groot huis, geen klein raam.

That's the entire rule. One flowchart, two paths.

⚠️ After the noun (predicative), NEVER add -e:
Het huis is groot. (not 'grote')
De tuin is mooi. (not 'mooie')
Only BEFORE nouns does -e apply.

steps.q: 'De tuin is mooi.' — why no -e on 'mooi'?
opts: The adjective is AFTER the noun
steps.ans: The adjective is AFTER the noun

steps.title: Schoonmaken & Opruimen
steps.desc: Dutch housework verbs are almost ALL separable. The verb splits in two: the prefix flies to the end of the sentence. You've seen this with 'aankleden' and 'wakker worden'. Now: the full system, with housework as the playground.
goals: Word order: prefix at the end

steps.title: ✂️ Separable Verbs: The Split
steps.text:
Some Dutch verbs have a prefix that detaches in main clauses:

opruimen (to tidy up):
Ik ruim de kamer op. = I tidy up the room.

schoonmaken (to clean):
Ik maak de keuken schoon. = I clean the kitchen.

The prefix (op, schoon, aan, uit, mee...) goes to the END.
The verb stays in position 2.

It's like English 'pick up', 'clean out', 'turn on':
'I pick the toys UP.' — same logic!

⚠️ With modals, the verb DOESN'T split:
Ik moet de kamer opruimen. (infinitive stays whole)
Ik wil de keuken schoonmaken. (no split)

steps.en: to tidy up / to clean up
steps.note:
op + ruimen = to tidy up.
'Op' goes to the end: 'Ik ruim ... op.'
Very common word in Dutch households.

schoon (clean) + maken (to make) = to clean.
The prefix 'schoon' goes to the end.
'Zij maakt de keuken schoon.'

steps.en: to do the dishes / to wash up
steps.exampleEn: I do the dishes every evening.
steps.note:
af + wassen = to wash off/away.
German cognate: abwaschen.
⚠️ 'Ik was af' vs 'Ik was me' — context!

stof (dust) + zuigen (to suck) = to vacuum.
Literally: 'dust-sucking'.
Technically separable but rarely split in practice.

open + maken = to open (make open).
Alternative: 'openen' (non-separable).
'Maak het raam open' is more conversational.

dicht (closed/shut) + doen (to do) = to close.
Dicht goes to the end: 'Doe de deur dicht.'
'Doen' you learned in Unit 3!

steps.title: 📐 Split vs No Split
steps.text:
MAIN CLAUSE (present tense): the verb SPLITS.
Ik ruim de kamer op.
Zij maakt de keuken schoon.
Hij wast de borden af.

WITH A MODAL: the verb stays WHOLE (infinitive).
Ik moet de kamer opruimen.
Zij wil de keuken schoonmaken.
Hij kan de borden afwassen.

The rule: only the CONJUGATED verb splits.
Infinitives (after modals) stay in one piece.

This is the same in German:
Ich räume das Zimmer auf. (splits)
Ich muss das Zimmer aufräumen. (whole)

steps.note:
'Elke dag' = every day. 'Elke week' = every week.
'Elke ochtend' = every morning.
Always followed by a singular noun.

steps.q: 'Ik ruim de kamer op.' — where does 'op' go?

steps.hint: She makes the kitchen clean (stam + t)

steps.q: With a modal verb, separable verbs:
steps.hint: Ik MOET de kamer opruimen (no split)

steps.s: Ik was elke avond {1}. Ik moet de kamer {2}.
Hij ___ elke dag de woonkamer schoon.
steps.hint: In main clause: verb in pos 2, prefix at end

steps.q: 'Ik maak het raam open.' — which part is the prefix?

steps.title: Gefeliciteerd!
steps.desc: The Dutch birthday is one of the most unique cultural experiences in Europe. You congratulate EVERYONE — the birthday person AND their family. You sit in a circle. There is cake. This lesson teaches the vocabulary and the survival rules for a Dutch verjaardag.

steps.note:
De-word. Literally: ver- + jaar + dag = anniversary day.
'Jaar' = year, 'dag' = day.
Very different from German 'Geburtstag' (birth-day).

steps.exampleEn: Happy birthday! / Congratulations on your birthday!
steps.note:
The longest word you've learned so far.
Used for birthdays, achievements, EVERYTHING.
'Gefeliciteerd!' alone is enough.

De-word. Cognate of English 'tart'.
At Dutch birthdays: always taart. Always.
Often: appeltaart (apple pie).

Het-word. Borrowed from French.
Also: het kado (simplified spelling — both correct).
'Voor jou' = for you.

Same root as English 'for' and German 'für'.
Also means 'before' (in time):
'voor drie uur' = before three o'clock.

Adjective that means 'having a birthday TODAY'.
English has no equivalent!
'Ik ben jarig.' = It's my birthday.
'Zij is jarig.' = It's her birthday.

steps.title: 🇳🇱 The Dutch Birthday Circle
steps.text:
The Dutch birthday party is unlike any other in the world.

THE CIRCLE: Everyone sits in a circle of chairs.
Not standing. Not mingling. A CIRCLE.
You sit, you talk to the people next to you, you eat cake.

THE CONGRATULATIONS: You congratulate EVERYONE.
Not just the birthday person, but their family too:
'Gefeliciteerd met je moeder!' = Congratulations with your mother!
'Gefeliciteerd met je zoon!' = Congratulations with your son!

Yes. You congratulate the mother of the birthday person.
And the sister. And the neighbour. And the colleague.

THE CAKE: Usually made by the birthday person.
Yes — the birthday person PROVIDES the cake.
At work: you bring your own taart on YOUR birthday.

THE CALENDAR: de verjaardagskalender.
A calendar in the toilet listing everyone's birthday.
Every Dutch home has one. In. The. Toilet.

deepDive.title: Why congratulate the family?
deepDive.text:
This confuses every foreigner.

'Gefeliciteerd met je man!'
= Congratulations with your husband!
(said to the wife of the birthday person)

The logic: the birthday is a happy event for the whole family.
By congratulating the family members, you acknowledge
that this is THEIR happy event too.

It's communal joy. It's very Dutch.

The correct response when congratulated:
'Dank je wel!' = Thank you!
Even if it's not YOUR birthday.
Because it's your family's special day.

This is one of those things you just have to experience.

steps.example: De verjaardagskalender hangt in het toilet.
steps.exampleEn: The birthday calendar hangs in the toilet.
steps.note:
Compound noun: verjaardag + s + kalender.
Hangs in the toilet = 100% true, 100% Dutch.
Every family member's birthday is on it.

steps.example: Er zijn twaalf kaarsen op de taart.
steps.exampleEn: There are twelve candles on the cake.
steps.note:
De-word. Plural: kaarsen.
The Dutch love candles — not just on cakes.
Every woonkamer has candles. Gezelligheid!

steps.q: At a Dutch birthday, you congratulate:
Who brings cake on a Dutch birthday at work?

steps.s: De {1} hangt in het toilet. Er zijn twaalf {2} op de taart.

steps.hint: Every Dutch home. In the toilet.

steps.title: Buren & Bezoek
steps.desc: Visiting someone's home in the Netherlands has its own unwritten rules. This lesson brings together everything from Unit 5 — family, house, possessives, reflexives, adjectives — in a natural social scenario: a neighbour comes over for coffee.

steps.note:
De-word. Plural: buren.
Also: de buurman (male), de buurvrouw (female).

steps.exampleEn: The neighbors are coming to visit.
steps.note:
'Op bezoek komen' = to come visit.
'Op bezoek gaan' = to go visit.
'Bezoek' = visit (het bezoek).

Separable: binnen + komen.
'Kom binnen!' = Come in! (invitation)
The most welcoming phrase in Dutch.

You learned this in Unit 4.
'Ga zitten' = Go sit / Have a seat.
Used when welcoming someone into your home.

steps.exampleEn: Would you like something to drink?
steps.note:
Very useful small word.
'Iets drinken?' = Something to drink?
'Iets eten?' = Something to eat?

steps.also: also means 'nice/pleasant' in general: 'lekker weer' = nice weather, 'lekker slapen' = sleep well
steps.note:
The most Dutch word in existence.
Meaning 1: tasty (food/drink)
Meaning 2: pleasant/nice (weather, sleep, everything)
'Lekker!' on its own = 'Yum!' or 'Nice!'

THE untranslatable Dutch word.
Cosy + social + warm + pleasant + togetherness.
A candle-lit room with friends and cake = gezellig.
A lonely walk in the rain = NOT gezellig.

steps.title: 🏡 A Neighbour Comes Over: The Dialogue
steps.text:
A: (rings doorbell)
B: Hallo! Kom binnen! = Hello! Come in!
A: Bedankt! Wat een mooi huis! = Thanks! What a nice house!
B: Dank je wel. Ga zitten! = Thank you. Have a seat!
B: Wil je iets drinken? = Would you like something to drink?
A: Ja, graag! Een kopje koffie. = Yes please! A cup of coffee.
B: Met melk? = With milk?
A: Nee, zwart, alsjeblieft. = No, black, please.
B: (gives coffee) Alsjeblieft!
A: Dank je. Mmm, lekker! = Thanks. Mmm, tasty!
B: Wil je een stukje taart? = Want a piece of cake?
A: Ja! Het is heel gezellig hier. = Yes! It's very gezellig here.

This is THE Dutch home visit. Coffee + cake + gezelligheid.

deepDive.title: Visiting rules in the Netherlands
deepDive.text:
1. ALWAYS accept the coffee. Refusing is awkward.
2. The host offers — you don't help yourself.
3. ONE cookie/slice. Wait to be offered a second.
   The Dutch are generous but structured.
4. Don't overstay. 1.5-2 hours is a good visit.
5. Comment on the house: 'Wat een mooi huis!'
   This is expected and appreciated.
6. 'Gezellig' is the highest compliment you can give.
   'Het was heel gezellig!' = It was really pleasant!
   Say this when leaving. Always.

steps.note:
Diminutive of 'stuk' (piece).
'Een stukje taart' = a (small) piece of cake.
Using the diminutive is polite — you're not asking for a lot.

steps.hint: There IS no single English word

steps.q: When visiting a Dutch home, you should:
opts: accept the coffee and compliment the house
steps.ans: accept the coffee and compliment the house

steps.hint: Something to drink? A cup of coffee?

steps.q: 'Lekker' can mean all of these EXCEPT:

steps.s: Het was heel ___ hier! (said when leaving)
steps.hint: The highest Dutch compliment for a visit

steps.title: Unit 5: Alles Samen
steps.desc: Family, house, reflexive verbs, adjective agreement, separable verbs, birthdays, visiting — all of Unit 5 mixed together. The scenario: a family birthday gathering at oma's house.

steps.title: 📋 What You Know Now
steps.text:
After Unit 5 you can:

• Name all family members
• Use ALL possessives (mijn, jouw, zijn, haar, uw, ons/onze, jullie, hun)
• Describe your morning with reflexive verbs
• Add -e to adjectives correctly (and know the ONE exception)
• Use separable verbs (split in main clause, whole with modals)
• Survive a Dutch birthday
• Visit someone's home politely
• Use 'gezellig' and 'lekker' like a local

steps.q: In 'Ik kleed me aan', which part is the separable prefix?
At a Dutch birthday, the birthday person:

steps.hint: Separable: verb in pos 2, prefix at end

steps.s: Ik moet de kamer {1}. Ik ruim mijn kamer {2}.
steps.hint: With modal: whole. Main clause: splits.
